summary:
The Sesklo culture, centered on the site of Sesklo near Volos in Thessaly, Greece, flourished from approximately 6500 to 4400 BC and represents one of the earliest and most developed Neolithic cultures of Europe. Sesklo communities built substantial mud-brick houses arranged in planned settlements, some of which had a central megaron-style structure that may have served communal or administrative functions. The site of Sesklo itself shows evidence of repeated occupation and growth, suggesting a degree of settlement permanence rare for this period in Europe.

The culture is particularly noted for its painted pottery, featuring bold geometric designs in red on a white or cream ground — one of the most sophisticated Neolithic ceramic traditions in the Aegean region. Sesklo farmers cultivated emmer wheat, einkorn, and barley, and kept sheep, goats, and cattle. Their settlements represent an early phase of the broader Neolithic spread across Greece and southeastern Europe, providing material and cultural connections that link Anatolia with the emerging farming communities of the Balkans and the wider European interior.
